event: Saharan peoples create the rock art of the Acacus Mountains, depicting a then-green region rich with wildlife and herding cultures.
Neolithic pastoral communities herd cattle across a wetter Sahara before progressive desertification pushes populations toward the coast and oases.
Berber (Amazigh) tribes, including the Libu people who gave the land its name, are established across the region and raid neighboring Egypt.
Shoshenq I, a leader of Libyan (Libu/Meshwesh) descent, founds Egypt's 22nd Dynasty and rules from the Nile Delta.
Greek colonists from Thera found Cyrene in eastern Libya, anchoring the prosperous region of Cyrenaica and its Pentapolis cities.
The Persian Achaemenid Empire under Cambyses II extends its authority over Cyrenaica.
Cyrene submits to Alexander the Great, drawing the region into the Hellenistic world.
Ptolemy I incorporates Cyrenaica into Ptolemaic Egypt after Alexander's death.
Rome destroys Carthage, ending Punic influence and opening the western Libyan region of Tripolitania to Roman power.
Cyrenaica is bequeathed to Rome by its last Ptolemaic ruler, Ptolemy Apion, and later organized as a Roman province.
Septimius Severus, born in Leptis Magna in Tripolitania, becomes Roman emperor and lavishly rebuilds his home city.
A massive earthquake and tsunami devastate the coastal cities of Cyrenaica, including Apollonia and Cyrene.
The Vandals sweep across North Africa, soon taking control of Tripolitania.
Byzantine forces under Belisarius reconquer the region from the Vandals, restoring Roman imperial rule.
Arab Muslim armies under Amr ibn al-As conquer Cyrenaica and Tripolitania, beginning the gradual Arabization and Islamization of Libya.
The Aghlabid dynasty governs Tripolitania as semi-autonomous rulers under the Abbasid Caliphate.
The Fatimids rise to power in North Africa and bring the Libyan coast under their Shia caliphate.
The Banu Hilal and Banu Sulaym Arab tribes migrate into Libya, decisively Arabizing the population and language.
The Norman Kingdom of Sicily briefly seizes Tripoli and other coastal towns.
Spain captures Tripoli, later handing it to the Knights of St. John in 1530.
Ottoman corsairs under Turgut Reis take Tripoli, beginning more than three centuries of Ottoman influence over Libya.
Ahmed Karamanli founds the semi-independent Karamanli dynasty in Tripoli, presiding over a corsair economy of privateering and ransom.
The First Barbary War begins as the United States resists tribute demands from Tripoli over Mediterranean shipping.
U.S. forces and allies capture Derna in the celebrated 'shores of Tripoli' campaign, helping end the First Barbary War.
The Ottomans depose the Karamanlis and impose direct rule over Tripolitania and Cyrenaica.
The Senussi Sufi order is founded and spreads across Cyrenaica, becoming a major religious, social, and later political force.
Italy invades Ottoman Libya in the Italo-Turkish War, beginning the brutal Italian colonial conquest.
The Treaty of Ouchy formally cedes Libya from the Ottoman Empire to Italy, though armed Libyan resistance continues.
Italy unites Tripolitania and Cyrenaica into a single colony, intensifying its campaign of pacification.
Italian forces capture and hang resistance leader Omar Mukhtar after a long guerrilla war and the use of concentration camps that killed tens of thousands.
Allied forces drive the Axis from Libya during World War II, placing the territory under British and French military administration.
Libya gains independence as the United Kingdom of Libya under King Idris I, the first state created by the United Nations.
Libya joins the United Nations as a sovereign member state.
Large oil reserves are discovered, transforming impoverished Libya into a major petroleum exporter.
Libya abolishes its federal structure, becoming a unitary kingdom, and is a founding member of the Organization of African Unity the same year.
Muammar Gaddafi leads a bloodless coup that overthrows King Idris and proclaims the Libyan Arab Republic.
Gaddafi's government evacuates the British and American military bases and expels much of the Italian and Jewish population.
Gaddafi declares the Great Socialist People's Libyan Arab Jamahiriya, his idiosyncratic system of 'direct popular rule.'
The United States bombs Tripoli and Benghazi in retaliation for Libyan links to terrorism, killing dozens including Gaddafi's adopted daughter.
The bombing of Pan Am Flight 103 over Lockerbie, Scotland, kills 270 people and is later attributed to Libyan agents.
The UN imposes sanctions on Libya for refusing to hand over the Lockerbie bombing suspects.
Libya accepts responsibility for Lockerbie, agrees to compensate victims, and renounces its weapons of mass destruction programs, leading to the lifting of sanctions.
An anti-Gaddafi uprising erupts during the Arab Spring; NATO intervenes and rebels capture and kill Gaddafi, ending his 42-year rule.
Libya holds its first free national elections for a General National Congress, but an attack on the U.S. mission in Benghazi kills the ambassador.
Rival governments and militias plunge Libya into a second civil war, splitting the country between Tripoli and the east.
The UN-brokered Libyan Political Agreement establishes the Government of National Accord, though it fails to unify the country.
Khalifa Haftar's eastern forces launch a major offensive on Tripoli, escalating the conflict with foreign-backed proxies on both sides.
A UN-supported ceasefire leads to a unified interim Government of National Unity tasked with steering Libya toward national elections.
Storm Daniel triggers catastrophic dam collapses in Derna, killing thousands and displacing tens of thousands in one of the deadliest floods in modern North African history.
